skill.name: routeros-command-tree
skill.description: RouterOS command tree introspection via /console/inspect API. Use when: building tools that parse RouterOS commands, generating API schemas from RouterOS, working with /console/inspect, mapping CLI commands to REST verbs, traversing the RouterOS command hierarchy, or when the user mentions inspect, command tree, RAML, or OpenAPI generation for RouterOS.
skill.summary: Technical documentation for RouterOS /console/inspect API and command tree structure
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: All 138 static analysis findings are false positives from TypeScript code examples embedded in documentation. The file contains only technical documentation about RouterOS APIs with no executable code. No security risks detected.

content.value_statement: Understanding the RouterOS command hierarchy is complex. This skill provides complete documentation for the /console/inspect API, enabling you to explore command trees programmatically and generate API schemas.
actual_capabilities: Document the /console/inspect REST API endpoint and request types
Explain RouterOS command tree structure (dir, path, cmd, arg nodes)
Provide TypeScript code examples for tree traversal patterns
Map CLI commands to HTTP verbs for REST API usage
Describe RAML and OpenAPI schema generation from command trees
List dangerous paths that crash the REST server during syntax queries
limitations: Documentation only - contains no executable code or API functionality
Full tree traversal takes many minutes against live routers
Command tree varies between RouterOS versions and installed packages

use_cases.title: Generate API Schemas
use_cases.description: Create RAML or OpenAPI specifications from RouterOS command trees for API documentation and client generation.
use_cases.target_user: API developer building RouterOS integration tools

use_cases.title: Build Network Automation Tools
use_cases.description: Parse RouterOS CLI commands and map them to REST API calls for automated network configuration management.

use_cases.title: Explore Command Hierarchies
use_cases.description: Navigate the complete RouterOS command structure programmatically to discover available configuration options.

prompt_templates.title: Explain Command Tree Basics
prompt_templates.prompt: What are the four node types in the RouterOS command tree and what does each one represent?

prompt_templates.title: List Children at Path
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the TypeScript code to list all children under the /ip/address path using the /console/inspect API.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer writing code to explore RouterOS commands

prompt_templates.title: Generate REST API Mapping
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I map the CLI commands 'add', 'set', 'remove', and 'print' to HTTP verbs for the RouterOS REST API?
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer converting CLI commands to REST API calls

prompt_templates.title: Avoid Server Crashes
prompt_templates.prompt: Which path segments should I avoid when querying syntax descriptions with /console/inspect to prevent crashing the RouterOS REST server?
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer implementing safe tree traversal logic

output_examples.input: What are the four node types in the RouterOS command tree?
output_examples.output: The RouterOS command tree has four node types: dir (directory containing child paths like /ip or /system), path (navigable level with commands like /ip/address), cmd (executable action like add, set, print, remove), and arg (parameter to a command like address= or interface=).

output_examples.input: How do I map the 'add' CLI command to a REST API call?
output_examples.output: The 'add' CLI command maps to HTTP PUT (not POST). For example, creating a new IP address uses PUT /rest/ip/address with a JSON body containing the address, interface, and other parameters. This is the opposite of many REST API conventions.

output_examples.input: Which paths should I avoid when querying syntax descriptions?
output_examples.output: Avoid querying syntax for argument nodes inside subtrees containing: where, do, else, rule, command, or on-error. These are RouterOS scripting constructs that crash the HTTP server when you request syntax descriptions for their arguments. Enumerating children with the 'child' request is safe even in these paths.

best_practices: Skip syntax lookups for argument nodes inside dangerous paths (where, do, else, rule, command, on-error) to prevent REST server crashes
Use the INSPECTFILE environment variable to cache command tree data during development and avoid repeated live router queries
Convert path arrays to comma-separated strings using Array.toString() for /console/inspect API calls
When generating API schemas, map CLI commands to HTTP verbs: get→GET, add→PUT, set→PATCH, remove→DELETE
anti_patterns: Do not query syntax descriptions for argument nodes inside scripting construct paths (where, do, else, rule, command, on-error) as this crashes the RouterOS REST server
Do not use POST for creating new RouterOS resources - use PUT instead (add command = PUT, not POST)
Do not perform full tree traversals on every operation - cache results in inspect.json for offline use
Do not use slash-separated paths in /console/inspect requests - use comma-separated format instead

faq.question: What is the /console/inspect API?
faq.answer: The /console/inspect API is a REST endpoint available on RouterOS 7.x that lets you programmatically explore the entire command hierarchy. It supports requests like 'child' to list children, 'syntax' to get help text, and 'highlight' for syntax highlighting data.

faq.question: Why are there 138 static analysis security findings?
faq.answer: All 138 findings are false positives from TypeScript code examples embedded in the documentation. The file contains only technical documentation with no executable code - the patterns detected are fetch() calls and array methods in code examples showing how to use the RouterOS API.

faq.question: How do I represent paths in /console/inspect requests?
faq.answer: Paths use comma-separated segments, not slashes. Root is an empty string, /ip is 'ip', /ip/address is 'ip,address', and /ip/address/add is 'ip,address,add'. You can generate this format from JavaScript arrays using Array.toString().

faq.question: Which HTTP verb creates new resources in RouterOS REST API?
faq.answer: Use PUT to create new resources (the 'add' CLI command maps to PUT). This is the opposite of many REST API conventions where POST creates resources. PATCH updates existing resources ('set' command), and DELETE removes them ('remove' command).

faq.question: Why does querying certain paths crash the RouterOS REST server?
faq.answer: Querying syntax descriptions for argument nodes inside RouterOS scripting construct paths (where, do, else, rule, command, on-error) terminates the HTTP server process. Enumerating children with the 'child' request is safe even in these paths - only syntax lookups for arguments crash.

faq.question: Can I use this skill without a live RouterOS device?
faq.answer: Yes. Tools can parse inspect.json files offline without needing a live router. Set the INSPECTFILE environment variable and schema generators will use the cached file instead of querying a router. This is recommended for development and testing.
